summary: Paul of Tarsus (c. 5 - c. 64/65 CE) was a Greek-speaking Diaspora Jew and former Pharisee who, after an experience he understood as an encounter with the risen Jesus, became a missionary to non-Jews and the author of the earliest surviving Christian writings. His letters, several of them among the oldest documents in the New Testament, shaped later Christian thought on the law, grace, justification, and the inclusion of Gentiles.
description: Paul describes himself as a Jew of the tribe of Benjamin, circumcised on the eighth day, and, before his call, a Pharisee zealous for the ancestral traditions who persecuted the early Jesus movement (Galatians 1, Philippians 3). He reports that this changed through an experience he understood as a revelation of the risen Jesus, which he presents as his commissioning to proclaim Jesus among the Gentiles (Galatians 1:15-16); this self-understanding is his own attributed account and is described here, not asserted by the editor as a historical fact. Writing in Greek to communities he had founded or corresponded with around the northeastern Mediterranean, Paul addressed disputes over whether non-Jewish converts had to be circumcised and keep the Mosaic law, and argued that Gentiles are made right with God (justified) through faith in Christ apart from works of the law. Historical-critical scholarship distinguishes seven letters generally regarded as undisputedly written by Paul (Romans, 1 and 2 Corinthians, Galatians, Philippians, 1 Thessalonians, and Philemon) from six further letters attributed to him whose authorship is disputed or widely judged pseudonymous (Ephesians, Colossians, 2 Thessalonians, and the Pastoral Epistles, 1 and 2 Timothy and Titus); the undisputed letters are the primary evidence for his own thought, while the Acts of the Apostles supplies a later narrative of his career that scholars weigh critically against the letters. Much recent scholarship (the 'new perspective' associated with E. P. Sanders, James D. G. Dunn, and N. T. Wright, and the 'Paul within Judaism' reading of Paula Fredriksen and others) situates Paul firmly within the diversity of Second Temple Judaism and reads his mission as the ingathering of the nations to the God of Israel rather than as a departure from Judaism. How far Paul broke with or remained inside his ancestral tradition, and what he meant by the law, faith, and justification, remain actively debated, and this entry presents these as scholarly questions rather than settled results. The traditional date of his death, a martyrdom at Rome under Nero, is not recorded in the undisputed letters and rests on later tradition.
editorial_notes: Paul's self-understanding as one called by an encounter with the risen Jesus is presented as his own attributed account (per neutrality principles #1, #2, #4), following the exemplar treatment of confessional claims; the editor describes his theology of justification, grace, and the law as his attributed position and as a matter of ongoing scholarly debate, without adjudicating it. The undisputed-versus-disputed letter distinction and the critical use of Acts are reported as the standard findings of historical-critical scholarship, with the specific list of seven undisputed letters given as the scholarly consensus. Lifespan years (c. 5 - c. 64/65 CE) are approximate and as commonly reported; the date and manner of death rest on later tradition, noted as such. Sources triangulate three lenses per neutrality principles #8-9: (a) academic scholarship on Paul (E. P. Sanders; N. T. Wright; James D. G. Dunn), (b) the primary corpus (Paul's own undisputed letters and Acts), and (c) a contemporary scholar reading Paul within Judaism (Paula Fredriksen). No encyclopedias or tertiary sources are cited. Confidence is medium: sources verified to exist and be on-topic via publisher and library catalogues and peer-reviewed reviews, but relevant passages not read line-by-line. Relation targets that exist in the dataset: pharisees, jesus-of-nazareth, second-temple-judaism, justification, grace. The reference to council-of-jerusalem is to an entity authored in the same batch.

lifespan.note: c. 5 - c. 64/65 CE; both dates approximate. Born at Tarsus in Cilicia. The date and manner of death (traditionally a martyrdom at Rome under Nero, c. 64-65) are not attested in the undisputed letters and rest on later tradition.

relations.note: Paul identifies himself as a Pharisee before his call (Philippians 3:5), zealous for the ancestral traditions; reported as his own attributed self-description.
Paul understood his commissioning as an encounter with the risen Jesus (Galatians 1:15-16); presented as his attributed self-understanding, not asserted by the editor.

Paul is a participant in the apostolic meeting over Gentile circumcision and the law; his own account (Galatians 2) differs in emphasis from Acts 15. Forward reference to an entity authored in the same batch.
Much recent scholarship situates Paul within the diversity of Second Temple Judaism rather than outside it; the degree of continuity is debated.
Paul's letters, especially Galatians and Romans, are the primary source for the language of being justified through faith apart from works of the law; the meaning is actively debated in scholarship.
Paul gives grace (charis) a central place in his account of how God acts toward Jew and Gentile; reported as his attributed emphasis.

sources.citation: Sanders, E. P. Paul, the Law, and the Jewish People. Philadelphia: Fortress Press, 1983. Academic study of Paul's relation to the law and to his fellow Jews, foundational for the 'new perspective' on Paul; the outside-scholarship lens.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference, relevant passages not read line-by-line.

sources.citation: Dunn, James D. G. The Theology of Paul the Apostle. Grand Rapids, MI: William B. Eerdmans, 1998. A full-scale academic exposition of Paul's thought organized around Romans; a principal statement of the 'new perspective'.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference, relevant passages not read line-by-line.

sources.citation: Wright, N. T. Paul and the Faithfulness of God. Christian Origins and the Question of God, vol. 4. Minneapolis: Fortress Press, 2013. A large-scale academic study reading Paul's theology in light of Second Temple Judaism; a further 'new perspective' voice.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference, relevant passages not read line-by-line.

sources.citation: Fredriksen, Paula. Paul: The Pagans' Apostle. New Haven, CT: Yale University Press, 2017. Reads Paul as a first-century Jewish thinker whose Gentile mission belongs to a Jewish eschatological expectation of the turning of the nations; the 'Paul within Judaism' lens and the contemporary-scholar voice.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference, relevant passages not read line-by-line.

sources.citation: The undisputed Pauline letters (Romans, 1-2 Corinthians, Galatians, Philippians, 1 Thessalonians, Philemon) and the Acts of the Apostles. Primary sources: the letters are the earliest first-person evidence for Paul's self-understanding and thought, and Acts is the later narrative of his career weighed critically against them. Cited from The Greek New Testament, ed. Aland et al., 5th rev. ed. Stuttgart: Deutsche Bibelgesellschaft, 2014.
sources.license: Underlying texts public domain; critical edition proprietary. Specific passages (Galatians 1-2, Philippians 3) consulted for the attributed self-description; full corpus not read line-by-line.
